The surgeon uses the da Vinci system to identify the ureters, which appear as deep green structures, while the bladder is visible in front. The surgeon progressively dissects the bowel from the back of the uterus, creating space for excision of endometriosis. The bowel is carefully freed and isolated from the uterus.

The surgeon uses the da Vinci system to progressively peel off the peritoneum from the back of the cervix, sending the tissue for pathology analysis. The procedure involves shaving bowel serosal lesions, which are nodular and infiltrating, without penetrating the muscle layer. The ureter is isolated, and dye is injected to visualize it during the dissection of a left pelvic sidewall nodule.

The surgeon carefully preserves the nerve on the left-hand side to maintain bowel innervation and prevent chronic constipation. A deep nodule of endometriosis is visible on the back of the uterus, which is being progressively shaved off using a precise technique.

The surgeon uses the da Vinci system to identify the ureter's proximity and excises a nodule in the right perirectal space on top of the bowel serosa. The excision includes a small amount of perirectal fat.
The final view shows clean, live tissue with no visible fibrotic tissue, indicating a successful excision. The top of the uterus is barely visible, highlighting the extensive area of excision.
